findings.description: Inverted expiry comparison in validate_session: 'if session.expires_at > utcnow()' deletes and rejects STILL-VALID sessions and instead returns the user_id only for EXPIRED ones, so every fresh token fails auth while expired tokens stay valid forever.

LOCKOUT_MINUTES (15) is never used and no last-failure timestamp is stored or compared, so once failed_attempts >= MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS the account is locked permanently instead of for 15 minutes, with no automatic recovery.

Sessions are stored only in the per-instance in-memory dict self._sessions, so in the stated multi-worker web-service context tokens issued by one process are invisible to others and all sessions are lost on restart, silently breaking the login/validate contract.

rotate_token relies on validate_session and therefore inherits the line-105 inversion: it only renews already-expired tokens and rejects live ones, leaving sliding renewal non-functional from the caller's perspective.
SQL injection via f-string interpolation of email into the login query (also crashes on a legitimate email containing a quote); noted in passing, defer detail to critic-security.
find_user_by_email loads the entire users table and linear-scans in Python instead of using a parameterized WHERE email = ? query; noted in passing, defer to critic-performance.
